advaita.rendering: Śrī Bhagavān said: Once more, O Mahābāhu (mighty-armed), hear My supreme word (paramam vacah), that which reveals the unsurpassable reality (niratiśaya-vastu) — for you drink it like nectar (amrita-iva). I shall declare it again out of desire for your good (hita-kāmyayā).
advaita.anchor: Śaṅkara reads 'paramam' as 'that which illumines the unsurpassable reality' (niratiśayavastunaḥ prakāśakam); the repetition is warranted because Arjuna receives the word with such delight he is as if drinking nectar. The motivation is pure compassion for the disciple's liberation, not Arjuna's personal welfare in any worldly sense.
advaita.distinctive_claim: The supreme word is valuable precisely because it points to nirguṇa Brahman; Kṛṣṇa speaks again only because a fit disciple (adhikārī) who receives jñāna like nectar must be served without remainder.

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: Śrī Bhagavān said: Having heard of My greatness (māhātmya) and being delighted (prīyamāṇāya), O Mahābāhu — you who desire the good constituted by the birth and growth of devotion (bhakty-utpatti-vivṛddhi-rūpa-hita) — hear once more My supreme word that elaborates the full expanse of My greatness.
viśiṣṭādvaita.anchor: Rāmānuja specifies that 'hita' here is not abstract liberation but concretely the arising and flourishing of bhakti (bhakty-utpatti-vivṛddhirūpa-hitakāmanā); the hearer's delight is already a sign of nascent devotion. The word 'bhūyaḥ' signals that the prior chapters were preamble; the real object — Bhagavān's vibhūtis and yoga — is now to be unfolded fully.
viśiṣṭādvaita.distinctive_claim: Unlike Advaita, the 'good' (hita) aimed at is not jñāna-liberation but the deepening of relational devotion; Bhagavān expands His glory precisely to generate and nourish that devotion.

dvaita.rendering: Śrī Bhagavān said: Hear once more, O Mahābāhu — for you who attain satisfaction (santoṣam prāpnuvate) upon hearing. This entire chapter declares Hari's vibhūtis (manifestations of divine excellence) and the principle that certain beings owe their distinctiveness (viśeṣaṇa-kāraṇatva) to Him, for the purpose of upāsanā (meditative worship).
dvaita.anchor: Madhva's brief comment orients the entire chapter programmatically: the chapter is not lyrical praise but a scriptural foundation for upāsanā — knowing Hari as the inner cause of all excellence enables correct devotional meditation. Arjuna's satisfaction on hearing (śrutvā santoṣaṃ prāpnuvate) marks him as a qualified worshipper.
dvaita.distinctive_claim: Madhva reads 'paramam vacah' through the lens of upāsanā-śāstra: the 'supreme word' is supreme because it establishes the eternal ontological superiority (viśeṣatva) of Hari over all jīvas — a point Advaita and Viśiṣṭādvaita each dissolve in different ways.

śuddhādvaita.rendering: Śrī Bhagavān said: Without being asked (anupṛṣṭaḥ), Hari himself once again declares His yoga-born splendour and vibhūtis for the increase of the bhakti already described — for the world that arises from sac-cid-ānanda is always of the nature of His Self (sadā-madātmakam). Hear, O Mahābāhu, My supreme word concerning the glory of My yoga and greatness (mad-yoga-vaibhava-jñāpana), which I shall speak out of desire for your good.
śuddhādvaita.anchor: Vallabha's subcommentary (anupṛṣṭaḥ — 'without being asked') is decisive: Kṛṣṇa volunteers the vibhūti-teaching out of pure grace-overflow (prasāda), not pedagogical obligation. The verse is embedded in Vallabha's larger doctrinal point that creation is the self-diffusion of sac-cid-ānanda — all vibhūtis are therefore real self-manifestations of Kṛṣṇa, not māyā-projections.
śuddhādvaita.distinctive_claim: Only in Śuddhādvaita does 'without being asked' become theologically loaded: it marks the difference between Kṛṣṇa-as-teacher and Kṛṣṇa-as-spontaneous-grace-giver; the universe itself is never anything other than His full (pūrṇa) self-expression.

bhakti.rendering: Śrī Bhagavān said: O Mahābāhu — you whose long arms are skilled in fulfilling sacred duty (svadharma-anuṣṭhāna) and in great service — hear once more My word, which is supreme in that it is grounded in the Supreme Self (paramātma-niṣṭham). I shall speak it to you who find delight (prīti) in drinking the nectar of My words, out of desire for your welfare.
bhakti.anchor: Śrīdhara provides a chapter-level frame: vibhūtis were sketched in chapters 7–9 (raso'ham apsu, aham kratuḥ, etc.) and will now be elaborated so that the devotee cultivates the vision of the Lord everywhere (sarvatra-īśvara-dṛṣṭi). 'Paramam' is glossed as paramātma-niṣṭham — the word is supreme because its subject matter is the Supreme Self, not because of rhetorical elevation.
bhakti.distinctive_claim: Śrīdhara's structural gloss (vibhūtis sketched in 7–9, now expanded in 10) gives the opening verse a literary-architectural weight absent in the other schools; the repetition (bhūyaḥ) is not mere cordiality but a signal of escalating doctrinal elaboration.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Śrī Bhagavān said: Once more, O Mahābāhu, hear My supreme, excellent word. Chapters 7–9 disclosed Bhagavān's tattva — both with upādhi (conditioned) and without upādhi (unconditioned) — and His vibhūtis were briefly noted there; now they must be elaborated for meditation (dhyāna) on the conditioned form, and the tattva itself restated for jñāna (knowledge of the unconditioned), since it is difficult to know (durvijñeyatvāt). I shall speak it to you who experience delight like drinking nectar, out of desire for your highest good.
advaita-bhakti.anchor: Madhusūdana explicitly structures the chapter as dual-purpose: expansion of vibhūtis for dhyāna-upāsanā of Bhagavān's sopādhika (conditioned) aspect, and re-exposition of tattva for nirūpādhika (unconditioned) jñāna. His invocatory verse praises the Kāśī-rāja guru who taught the rāja-vidyā-rāja-guhya of chapter 9 — signalling that Advaita jñāna and Kṛṣṇa-bhakti are not competing paths but nested registers.
advaita-bhakti.distinctive_claim: Madhusūdana alone insists the chapter serves both jñāna and bhakti simultaneously — the vibhūtis are pedagogically useful for a meditator who has not yet reached the unconditioned, while the same chapter's tattva-repetition serves the jñānin; no other school makes this explicit two-register claim at verse 10.1.

so_what_questions: Why does Kṛṣṇa speak 'again' (bhūyaḥ) without being asked — what does voluntary re-teaching reveal about the relationship between teacher and disciple in the Gītā's dramaturgy?

The schools split on what 'hita' (welfare/good) means here — liberation, devotion-growth, upāsanā-grounding, or jñāna. What is actually at stake in that disagreement, and which frame does your own practice privilege?
Śaṅkara's disciple drinks the word 'like nectar' — is receptivity itself a form of practice, and how does a practitioner cultivate that quality of listening?
Madhva frames the entire chapter as upāsanā-śāstra — does knowing that Hari is the 'cause of distinctiveness' in great beings change how you relate to excellence you encounter in others?

Vallabha insists Kṛṣṇa speaks without being prompted (anupṛṣṭaḥ). What is the experiential difference between teaching received as response to your question versus teaching that arrives before you knew to ask?

Madhusūdana says chapters 7–9 were preamble; the elaboration begins here. When in your own learning have you discovered that something you thought you understood was only a sketch — and what did the fuller picture cost you?
All six schools agree 'paramam vacah' is a superlative — but they disagree on what makes it supreme. What criterion for 'supreme knowledge' operates in your life, and is it one you chose consciously?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you return to a difficult conversation you thought you'd closed — a hard truth with a friend, a confrontation you walked away from — notice whether you are doing it for the other person's liberation (hita-kāmyayā) or for your own relief. Śaṅkara's Kṛṣṇa reopens not because Arjuna asked but because the disciple is ready to receive what the teacher has always been holding.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Track the moments in your day when you feel genuine delight upon hearing something — a piece of music, a sentence in a book, a person's honesty. Rāmānuja reads Arjuna's prīti (delight) as embryonic bhakti. Those moments of uncalculated joy at beauty or truth are not distractions from practice — they are the practice announcing itself.
everyday_applications.dvaita: When you meet someone who is genuinely excellent — a skilled craftsperson, an unusually kind person, an athlete at the limit of their form — Madhva's frame says: that excellence has a cause that is not reducible to their individual effort. Pause to register that. It does not diminish them; it places you in relation to something larger than the two of you.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha's key word is anupṛṣṭaḥ — unasked. Think of the last time someone gave you exactly what you needed without your having found words for it. That gift structure — spontaneous, unrequested, overflowing — is what Vallabha sees as the signature of grace. Practice receiving it without converting it immediately into obligation or debt.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's frame is sarvatra-īśvara-dṛṣṭi — the vision of the Lord everywhere. Choose one domain this week — your commute, your kitchen, a difficult colleague — and practice finding one point of excellence or beauty in it each day. The Tenth Chapter exists precisely to train that vision until it becomes natural rather than effortful.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana teaches that the same reality is available both through a form you can meditate on and through a formless truth you can know directly — and that most people need both at different seasons of their life. Notice which mode you are in right now: do you need a vivid image to anchor your attention, or does the image feel like a cage? Neither answer is permanent; the chapter is large enough for both.

self_assessment: Six schools rendered with genuine doctrinal divergence anchored directly in supplied bhāṣya prose; Śrīdhara payload was clean Sanskrit with no HTML artifacts; no bhāṣya was absent — all six commentators supplied substantive text.
